questionText: Explain the difference between an array and a linked list.
answerText: Arrays store elements contiguously in memory and provide O(1) random access. Linked lists use nodes with pointers and require O(n) time for access but allow O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions. Arrays are better for frequent access, linked lists for frequent insertions/deletions.

questionText: Implement a function to reverse a linked list.
answerText: Use three pointers: prev, curr, next. Iterate through the list, reversing the direction of pointers at each node. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

questionText: What is the time complexity of binary search?
answerText: Binary search has O(log n) time complexity because it divides the search space in half at each step. Space complexity is O(1) for iterative and O(log n) for recursive implementations.

questionText: Implement a solution to find the longest common subsequence (LCS) of two strings.
answerText: Use dynamic programming with a 2D table where dp[i][j] represents the LCS length for first i and j characters. If characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1, otherwise take the maximum of dp[i-1][j] and dp[i][j-1]. Time: O(m*n), Space: O(m*n).

questionText: Explain hash tables and how collision handling works.
answerText: Hash tables use a hash function to map keys to array indices. Collisions occur when multiple keys hash to the same index. Common collision handling methods: 1) Chaining: store multiple values in linked lists, 2) Open addressing: find another empty slot (linear/quadratic probing). Chaining is generally preferred.
